source_text: sources/comments/files/20260611-5015__WATT-Coalition/WATT Supplemental Comments Large Load Interconnection Submitted 6.10.26.txt
overall_summary: The WATT Coalition urges FERC to require consideration of Advanced Transmission Technologies in large load interconnection studies and go further by mandating RTO studies of grid-enhancing technology potential, directing operational planning reforms, and issuing guidance on ATT-specific incentives. The filing argues that restricting ATTs to the interconnection process alone will not unlock their full value, and that new Commission reporting requirements and regional forums can accelerate adoption across all regions.

quotes.text: requiring ATTs to be evaluated in interconnection processes is valuable, it is not sufficient to enable large load interconnection at the fastest reasonable pace and lowest possible cost.
quotes.concern: ATT evaluation in interconnection studies alone is necessary but not enough to unlock full grid capacity

quotes.text: we agree with the CEBA's recommendation "that the Commission require ATTs—including dynamic line ratings—to be evaluated alongside traditional upgrades for large load interconnections, consistent with Order No. 2023, given the significant benefits identified for consumers and interconnection customers."
quotes.concern: Filer endorses requiring ATT evaluation alongside traditional upgrades in interconnection studies

quotes.text: The time has come and gone for voluntary or incentivized consideration of GETs. It is well documented that use of GETs delivers cost reductions and cost avoidance to the benefit of consumers nationwide. Despite this, relatively few projects have been implemented.
quotes.concern: Voluntary GETs adoption has failed; mandatory requirements are overdue

quotes.text: accelerated ATT adoption is more likely to speed the interconnection of new large loads to constrained transmission systems rather than frustrate speed to power goals.
quotes.concern: ATT requirements would accelerate, not hinder, speed-to-power objectives

quotes.text: The fastest action that can be taken to identify potential untapped grid capacity is large-scale studies by RTOs. RTOs have sufficient data on grid constraints and electricity prices to use publicly available DLR datasets and/or software from the National Laboratory of the Rockies (NLR) to quickly determine the likely magnitude of value from DLR.
quotes.concern: RTO-level DLR studies using existing data are the fastest path to identifying untapped capacity

quotes.text: The transparency envisioned by these reporting requirements is also critical to ensuring just and reasonable rates by identifying opportunities to maximize the use of our existing grid, to the benefit of ratepayers across the country, and to ensure our investments in new transmission are efficient and cost effective—key components of just and reasonable rates.
quotes.concern: GETs reporting requirements serve ratepayer protection and just-and-reasonable-rate obligations

quotes.text: very few, if any, transmission owners have employees in engineering or operations roles where one of their primary job responsibilities is to ensure efficient grid operation.
quotes.concern: Transmission owners lack dedicated staff for grid efficiency, creating a structural gap GETs could fill

quotes.text: We urge the Commission to act, through a policy statement or order directing reports, to encourage transmission owners and RTOs to develop processes focused on improving grid efficiency. These processes could unlock latent grid capacity in the timescales needed to win the AI race–the next two to three years—as well as to address the growing affordability crisis.
quotes.concern: Commission action on operational planning processes can unlock capacity within two to three years

quotes.text: The Commission's record on ATT deployments and obstacles to deployments is several years old. The record in Docket No. RM-24-6-000 on the Implementation of Dynamic Line Ratings is largely from before 2024.
quotes.concern: FERC's existing record on ATT deployments is outdated and needs refreshing

quotes.text: we suggest that the Commission direct reports and invite further comments in an open or new docketed proceeding, focusing in particular on transmission owners and RTOs to refresh the Commission's record around the state of ATT adoption.
quotes.concern: Commission should open a new proceeding to collect updated information from transmission owners and RTOs

quotes.text: we encourage FERC to issue a policy statement with further guidance on what kinds of incentives for ATTs projects might be just and reasonable under the existing requirements of Order No. 679.
quotes.concern: FERC should clarify the incentive options available to transmission owners that deploy ATTs

quotes.text: The WATT Coalition has proposed a federal shared savings incentive that allows transmission owners to profit based on a portion of the congestion cost savings from GETs deployments. This directly links utility profits to consumer benefits and operational efficiency.
quotes.concern: A shared-savings incentive would align transmission owner profits with consumer benefit from GETs

quotes.text: we urge the Commission to encourage all RTOs to host forums to investigate the opportunity for ATTs and other solutions to increase capacity and improve affordability and how to integrate ATTs into all aspects of an RTO's processes.
quotes.concern: Regional forums hosted by RTOs would accelerate cross-utility knowledge sharing on ATTs

quotes.text: ATTs can deliver meaningful increases in transmission capacity to facilitate the interconnection of large load in less than three years. While recent deployments by PG&E, Georgia Power and others validate this potential, utility adoption remains limited.
quotes.concern: ATTs can deliver capacity gains within three years but adoption has not kept pace with demonstrated potential

bins.name: Require ATT study in interconnection
bins.description: The filer supports requiring ATT evaluation in large load interconnection studies, consistent with Order No. 2023 and CEBA's recommendation. It argues this step is necessary but not sufficient, and calls for broader RTO-level studies of DLR and topology optimization potential as well.

bins.name: ATTs reduce costs for ratepayers
bins.description: The filer argues that ATTs lower congestion costs, improve investment efficiency, and protect ratepayers. It cites Basin Electric's DLR deployment recovering capital costs in 38 days and saving over $20 million in 2024, and contends that reporting requirements on GETs potential are essential to ensuring just and reasonable rates.

bins.name: Operational planning reform needed
bins.description: The filer contends that transmission owners currently lack staff and processes dedicated to grid efficiency, and urges the Commission to direct operational planning reforms. It recommends monthly, quarterly, and yearly efficiency analyses that incorporate GETs, arguing these can unlock latent capacity within two to three years.

bins.name: Voluntary adoption has failed
bins.description: The filer, quoting Industrial Customer Organizations, argues that voluntary GETs consideration has produced too little adoption despite well-documented benefits. It contends the Commission must take a more aggressive posture and require GETs where cost-effective.

bins.name: ATTs accelerate, not slow, interconnection
bins.description: The filer directly rebuts the argument that ATT requirements would frustrate speed-to-power goals, arguing the opposite: ATTs on constrained systems speed up large load interconnection. Recent utility deployments demonstrate capacity gains achievable in under three years.

bins.name: RTO studies and reporting requirements
bins.description: The filer recommends that the Commission require RTOs to study DLR and topology optimization potential using existing grid data, and require transmission owners to report investment efficiency metrics. These reporting requirements would inform future rulemakings, including the pending DLR implementation docket.

bins.name: New grid efficiency processes at utilities
bins.description: The filer proposes specific monthly, quarterly, and yearly operational planning processes for transmission owners and RTOs, including congestion intervention thresholds, DLR deployment timelines, and comprehensive three-year efficiency studies. It argues the Commission should encourage these through a policy statement or directed reports.

bins.name: Commission's ATT record is outdated
bins.description: The filer notes that the Commission's record on ATT deployments and barriers dates largely from before 2024, and recommends directing reports and inviting comments from transmission owners and RTOs to update the record. The filer provides a list of specific questions to structure that information gathering.

bins.name: ATT-specific incentive guidance needed
bins.description: The filer urges FERC to issue a policy statement clarifying what incentives for ATT projects are permissible under Order No. 679. It identifies shared savings incentives and ROE adders as candidate structures and notes that no ATT projects have sought incentive treatment despite over $80 billion in total incentive applications.

bins.name: RTO-hosted forums for knowledge sharing
bins.description: The filer urges the Commission to encourage RTOs to host regional forums on ATT integration and to convene a FERC technical conference in 2027 to hear from all regions. The goal is to accelerate cross-utility learning and best practice adoption.

bins.name: PJM region ATT adoption gaps
bins.description: The filer notes that only Dominion Energy among PJM transmission owners has significant DLR adoption planned, and that PJM Transmission Owners oppose ATT requirements. It argues Commission action is needed to overcome regional resistance.

bins.name: MISO region GETs opportunity
bins.description: The filer cites MISO data showing that ambient and emergency ratings could have saved $168 million in real-time congestion costs in Winter 2023, and notes MISO has taken incremental steps including a congestion reconfiguration process.

bins.name: SPP topology optimization leadership
bins.description: The filer highlights SPP's tariff filing to implement economic topology optimization as a leading example of constructive RTO policy, citing studies showing potential congestion savings of $18 to $44 million annually in SPP's footprint.

bins.name: ISO-NE regional forum inclusion
bins.description: The filer recommends that all RTOs, including ISO-NE, host forums on ATT integration opportunities as part of a Commission-encouraged regional knowledge-sharing initiative.
